item.title: How Does AI Actually 'Think'?
item.dc:creator: admin
item.excerpt:encoded: Quick 5-minute starter: understand that AI predicts patterns rather than "thinking", and why hallucinations occur.

wp:postmeta.wp:meta_value: Quick 5-minute starter: understand that AI predicts patterns rather than "thinking", and why hallucinations occur.
a:3:{i:0;s:32:"Load slides 5–8 on the projector";i:1;s:28:"Arrange students into pairs";i:2;s:70:"Ensure all students have access to ChatGPT or similar tool (optional)";}

a:2:{i:0;a:3:{s:4:"term";s:30:"Large Language Model (LLM)";s:10:"definition";s:145:"AI trained on billions of texts to predict and generate human-like language. Works by predicting the most likely next word based on patterns, not by understanding meaning.";s:18:"key_stage_adapted";b:0;}i:1;a:3:{s:4:"term";s:13:"Hallucination";s:10:"definition";s:95:"When AI confidently generates false or made-up information. Happens because AI predicts 'likely' text, not verified facts.";s:18:"key_stage_adapted";b:0;}}

a:3:{i:0;a:2:{s:9:"objective";s:50:"Understand that AI predicts patterns rather than 'thinking'";s:10:"assessable";b:1;}i:1;a:2:{s:9:"objective";s:66:"Recognise the difference between pattern prediction and understanding";s:10:"assessable";b:1;}i:2;a:2:{s:9:"objective";s:30:"Learn why AI 'hallucinations' occur";s:10:"assessable";b:1;}}

a:4:{i:0;a:6:{s:4:"step";i:1;s:6:"action";s:70:"Display Slide 5 (title) and ask: 'When you use ChatGPT, do you think it understands you?'";s:8:"duration";s:0:"";s:12:"resource_ref";s:7:"Slide 5";s:13:"student_action";s:0:"";s:11:"teacher_tip";s:0:"";}i:1;a:6:{s:4:"step";i:2;s:6:"action";s:66:"Show Slide 6 (debate question) and give students time to discuss in pairs";s:8:"duration";s:10:"60 seconds";s:12:"resource_ref";s:7:"Slide 6";s:13:"student_action";s:15:"Pair discussion";s:11:"teacher_tip";s:75:"Circulate and listen — note any students who say AI 'thinks' for the correction in step 4";}i:2;a:6:{s:4:"step";i:3;s:6:"action";s:50:"Display Slide 7 (facts about how AI works)";s:8:"duration";s:0:"";s:12:"resource_ref";s:7:"Slide 7";s:13:"student_action";s:0:"";s:11:"teacher_tip";s:0:"";}i:3;a:6:{s:4:"step";i:4;s:6:"action";s:60:"Show Slide 8 (key takeaways) and emphasise verification";s:8:"duration";s:0:"";s:12:"resource_ref";s:7:"Slide 8";s:13:"student_action";s:0:"";s:11:"teacher_tip";s:0:"";}}
When you ask ChatGPT a question, do you think it 'understands' you the way a human would?

a:3:{i:0;s:50:"What's the difference between knowing something and predicting it?";i:1;s:40:"Why might it matter if AI doesn't truly understand?";i:2;s:45:"How might knowing this change how you use AI tools?";}

a:6:{i:0;s:58:"No — AI processes text as mathematical patterns, not meaning";i:1;s:75:"It predicts what words are LIKELY to come next, based on its training data";i:2;s:58:"It has no experiences, feelings, or consciousness to draw from";i:3;s:60:"It can sound very confident and coherent while being completely wrong";i:4;s:75:"Understanding requires context, common sense, and real-world knowledge that AI lacks";i:5;s:75:"However, this doesn't mean AI isn't useful — it's a powerful tool when used appropriately";}

Students may anthropomorphise AI — gently correct 'it thinks' to 'it predicts'. Avoid being dismissive of AI's usefulness while explaining its limitations. Good analogy: AI is like a very sophisticated autocomplete, not a thinking being. This understanding helps students use AI more critically and effectively.

a:3:{s:7:"support";s:70:"Provide a simplified definition sheet. Pair weaker students with stronger partners.";s:6:"stretch";s:88:"Ask students to find a real example of an AI hallucination and explain why it happened.";s:4:"send";s:78:"Pre-teach key vocabulary before the lesson. Provide visual aids for abstract concepts.";}
a:1:{i:0;a:2:{s:8:"activity";s:78:"Research one real-world AI hallucination and present it in 2 minutes next lesson";s:4:"type";s:11:"next_lesson";}}
